utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But it's really cool to.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's really cool to meet you.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah, of course.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray

utterances.text: Yeah, absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know, we are super involved in careers and job hunting stuff in sort of all senses of the word and are generally, I would say, like, I try to allocate at least a little bit of time in my week to say, speak to folks that are, you know, I would say, fair to say, like considered trusted voices as far as providing career advice is concerned.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And also sort of get a sense from folks that I suspect are talking to a lot of job seekers either through mentorship or through sort of general discussions.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sort of getting a better sense of what pain points you're seeing out there and sort of, you know, the ways in which you've seen or you've tried to bring.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Bridge the gap between candidates and companies, if at all.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Sorry, can you restate the question?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So the, the.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I guess the, the best starting Point would be like, what are you hearing from like job seekers, the folks that you're mentoring, like are you seeing a lot of angst or difficulty trying to make it into their respective careers?

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: In terms of job seekers?

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I think people are having a really difficult time right now, especially earlier stage.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Folks.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Like people that I mentor through, through Tufts, people who are graduating soon or have graduated and looking for their first job in software.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Although I guess the same has been true for people who are much farther along in their careers.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And based on a presentation I actually saw by a Boston based recruiter from a recruiting consulting firm, he mentioned that there were actually very few positions for really senior technical like managers, directors and very few positions on the new grad side.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: But everybody in like the 3 to 7 to 10 year space was still seeing a lot of traction.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Although there's so many people getting dumped out of their seats all at the same time that it's kind of a scramble.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It really is kind of a scramble.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I think there are multiple forces at play and sort of each slice as you rightly identified, is sort of going through its own thing.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I think what's also unfortunate, something that you and I share in our backgrounds is career switching.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know, I noticed that you went joined CS from, from sort of geology, I think you'd said, or on your, on your LinkedIn.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The reason I bring that up is because I think that is one of the things that we're trying to solve is we actually think the career changers are oftentimes really great employees.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: They can be great hidden gems that actually give a startup or give a company a lot of optionality a new perspective.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The fact that they're willing to take a lower wage and therefore learn.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So there's a lot of upsides.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And our feeling is just that the systems and the processes we have to see or view candidates are shit.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know, depart, you know, part of my language.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But they just don't really look at the whole candidate in any way.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Nor do they really employ any reasonably good AI tech to like semantically evaluate what candidates are saying.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's, it's pretty when you dig into the tech, be it because the unit economics or, or lack of innovativeness, a lot of these companies are basically just sticking with a resume, sticking with video interviews, not really trying to learn more about the candidates.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And I think that's sort of where we're coming in, is trying to build a tool that not only helps candidates talk more about themselves and share more about themselves, but in the process provide us with the data to match them to better two jobs more effectively.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: So what does that.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: So are you building a interactive job board where people can put in a more rounded, I guess, experience?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah, pretty much.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So it's essentially instead of applying, like filling out a form, you're talking to our AI and reflecting on your experiences.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So it has the intrinsic benefit of helping you refine your thinking, you know, mull over things you may not have.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: There's a bunch of upsides.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It also takes what you shared and turns that into really high quality resume bullets and interview answers.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And so it provides all that sort of upside just by itself.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But our.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Our belief is that those aren't just nice to have.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We're not just doing it because we're trying to be nice to the candidate.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That is how you create a more sort of equal and meritocratic process is giving everyone the ability to put themselves forward effectively because that levels the field.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And the data that we've collected along the way helps us match people more effectively.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So it's like to get that data, you almost have to work with the candidate.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Even for really accomplished candidates that are, you know, easy hires oftentimes struggle because of the self advocacy issue.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So I guess that's our sort of broader point, is that the self advocacy part is what we're solving.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And in the process of solving that, we've also solved matching.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Okay, can we take a step back?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah, sure.

utterances.text: And then.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And now I'm actually, I have.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I'm not sure what.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: What this conversation is.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sure.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No, it's.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's totally an intro chat.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: By the way, am I on the wrong camera?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Okay, I'm looking at the right camera.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No, it's.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's totally an intro chat.

utterances.text: That's just sort of what we do.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That is just the.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's a pretty nitty gritty tool.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We started as a career coaching tool and ended up as a hiring tool.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's sort of our arc.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But we still want to work with candidates and we give out our tool for free because that's how we sort of build up our talent pool.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Okay, interesting.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so are you selling to companies that are hiring in kind of a traditional route recruiter pattern where you get some, like, compensation based on who they hire and their salary?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Pretty much, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We actually don't even want to be the substitute recruiter.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: We're effectively telling companies, hey, your ATS isn't really doing much for you.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's putting people in a kind of arbitrary order and you still have to read all of those resumes.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Why not hand out something that actually helps your folks get better and be better at sort of how they put themselves forward and also acts like a better signal of whether someone actually cares about your company.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's one of the things that we hear founders and employers wanting is, hey, we actually want folks that want to work here.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And it's hard to figure that out now that everyone is mass messaging emails and resumes and all of that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So the AI, it's basically the AI slot problem has hit, you know, the career space, which I'm sure you would have noticed as well over the last few months and years.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah, actually, somebody told me.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Somebody told me they were using this, like, auto apply thing.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And I just find that, I mean, on the one hand, it's understandable if you've been looking for a job for a really long time, and it doesn't matter what job you will because of.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: For, you know, for financial reasons.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: But then on the other hand, I feel like you're really not curating the conversations that you're having and like, it's not even about what you want anymore.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: So how can you guarantee your success if you're just like, haphazardously putting yourself out there with no real message or like, no, there's no story.

utterances.speaker: Logan Currie
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: It's just like, completely disconnected.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And your story, in theory, to be most effective, your story should be tailored to the audience.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Most of these auto apply tools won't really do that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: They'll sort of put in a nominal effort to tailor to the resume.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But it's an informational problem.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: If I have a finite amount of information about you, the candidate that is essentially limited to the resume, which is a pretty shallow data point, I can really only, like, either make things up or give you something super generic.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And that's sort of where most folks end up, is one or the other.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah, I mean, beyond information being tailored to the audience, I also think that the information is very much tailored to you and to what you want and what you care about.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And if it's a mismatch, then, like, sure, it's tailored to the company in the sense that, like, you're not a match.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And that's both good for you and for the company.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Absolutely.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I think with the auto apply tools, the problem is they're charging the candidate and the candidate has perceived benefit keyword being perceived when they see number of applications go up.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And it's only unfortunately, months later, after they spent somewhere in the order of 150 to $250 that they realize, oh, this was actually bad for me.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And what we're, what candidly I'm hearing more and more from founders is a lot of them aren't even taking, aren't even looking at inbound anymore.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: They're posting it for legal reasons, but they already have their referrals and, you know, sort of folks that they're recruiters.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And that's really unfortunate.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right, because it just leads to greater ossification, more of the sort of old boys club vibe, you know, whether people want to or not.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Now it's become an unfortunately sad, like, necessity almost.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah, yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's a, it's a, it's a really.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: There's a lot going on.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I guess that's true of every part of the world right now is there's.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: A lot going on, but that's true.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: What led you to, you know, you're, you're pretty out there as far as like advocacy and stuff are concerned.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: What are some issues or things that you are currently trying to put forward or messages that you're trying to get out there?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'm just curious.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Curious.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I don't think I'm trying to get a message out there.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I think I care tremendously about women in the workforce and just like representation in the workforce because I feel like so often tech is building for the tech pros and really the people who will benefit the most from tech are the people who are disconnected and like, don't have access to begin with.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: It's like the same thing with health care.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: You could build this like, you know, highly specific thing for the 1% of the population that is able to afford it.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: But like, really the biggest impact is going to be expanding access to care for the lay person.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Especially in the US with all the issues around insurance.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: So I feel like it's more about representation and everybody having a voice in tech and, and being able to bring their viewpoint and like, what they think is important, which will lead to us actually serving their communities.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Something that I've noticed that I actually hate.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And I don't know if this is just like AI bots, but if you look at like women in tech forums, like if you go to women in tech and Reddit or if you look online, more and more users are posting about how they're dropping out of tech and tech is horrible for women.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And I feel like it's this terrible.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I don't know if it's again, bots or if it's real people but it's turning into this weird echo chamber.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so what I want to put out there is like, no, actually, I think that you have a disproportionate number of negative feedback.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: You know, pieces of negative feedback because people are more likely to post when they have negative experiences.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: But there are also plenty of women who are thriving in tech and who are really good at it.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so I kind of, like, part of me wants to address that, but it's also.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I don't really understand how.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I guess something an interesting study someone could do.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Twitter just like, posted the country that people are posting from.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so I'm like, okay, or whatever.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: S like, oh, I would love for somebody to go and like, scrape all of the user data around negative sentiments for women in tech or like supposed women in tech posting negatively about themselves.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Is this like, where is that coming from?

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Is it real women or is it like some other, like.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I'm just.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: It's so mind.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The bot farms have gotten.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: The bot farms have gotten crazy and the.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And frankly, the attempts at destabilizing a society have gotten so intricate, you know, you can't really discount it.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I've certainly heard of even crazier things happening vis a vis, you know, folks taking up seats in community college for phantom students that they are using to then cultivate IDs identities specifically, or rather over the course of that time in community college.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And so you have these phantom graduates that they have ready to deploy for nefarious purposes around.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: So, you know, vectors of attacker are unfortunately a little bit upsetting.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: But to your point, that is incredibly discouraging that it's happening in a place where, you know, now is really a time when we want the opposite of that message getting out.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And I see as well why you sort of took pause for thought, because it's not just the message.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's like trying to create that change.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And bring about that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: What would you say are some of the hurdles that you are.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'm asking this purely from the perspective of maybe we can be more helpful or I could be more helpful.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: What are some of the hurdles or barriers that you reckon the folks you're mentoring are facing right now, either in terms of technology or.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Or just in terms of, like, access?

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I'm not really sure.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I mean, the first answer is going to be financial security.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: So, like, need a job because you have rent and food to pay for, like, financial security to succeed.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I think generally maybe in tech there's this, like, mass feeling of FOMO and There's a really strange disconnect because everybody is talking about AI, but the vast majority of people are not doing AI and the people who are talking about AI are also not the ones implementing it.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so it's like I've spoken to people at companies and like, yeah, we have layoffs because of AI, but we're not actually using AI.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so it's like, okay, how do you, how do you like connect those things?

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And so maybe like the lack, the missing tool is like, maybe we should be using AI, how do that?

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Or, or maybe it's, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah, I don't know.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Maybe people just feeling more people keep asking me like, oh, why don't you just go off and start your own thing.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: So I think something that has actually changed recently is people feel much more empowered to start their own organization, effort, project, whatever, you name it.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: And I think that's actually a good change.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Totally.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: I don't really know how to answer your question beyond that.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: No, it's a tough one, right?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: If I like, as you were sort of thinking, I was posing it to myself and I was like, what would I say?

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I'll probably just like respond to me like just getting someone to give a crap.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Or like to cut through the noise or do so successfully in, you know, one out of a 10, 10 attempts.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I think there is just too much information and too little time and even less attention to sort of go around and it's, it's leading to, I think unfortunately a lot of folks being squeezed out.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: For me, the reason it goes back to self advocacy is because it's the first folks that get squeezed out are the ones that aren't, you know, built to throw elbows and sort of make space for themselves.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Which tends to be disproportionately women, which tends to be disproportionately minorities or immigrants.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: You know, folks that on some psychological level have the programming of like, oh, if I just keep my head down and don't cause a fuss, like I will be, you know, I'll be good.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That's unfortunately not the case.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And so that is, is probably the, the, the problem that we are trying to solve that, that in, in a pretty unique way, I guess because we think that that is the central problem.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: It's like the resume is the symptom or the byproduct of the real problem, which is a lack of self understanding and self confidence.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: And how do we fix that in a scalable way is sort of like, you know, what interests me And Logan the most.

utterances.text: So beyond just a conversation, how are you actually addressing this in your product?

utterances.text: Or vibe check.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: That tends to be quite helpful because it illustrates pretty clearly.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Hey, these are the areas where you don't even have anything on your resume.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Right.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I can actually show you if you're, if you're keen, if you're more of a visual person.

utterances.speaker: Marianne Ray
utterances.text: Yeah, sure, that would be great.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: I am.

utterances.speaker: Vrijen Attawar
utterances.text: Sweet.

utterances.text: Yeah.
